critic_raw:
The methodology is carefully constructed and addresses many common pitfalls in financial ML experiments. However, I identify three genuine methodological issues with distinct root causes, plus one near-miss that resolves on reflection.

---

**Issue 1: Temporal leakage via stratified random split (not temporal split)**

The specific claim: stratified random sampling at the window level — preserving suspicious-case rate and risk-tier distribution — is sufficient partitioning for this experiment.

Why this might be wrong: In a rolling-window scheme over 36 months, a given account will appear in multiple overlapping or adjacent 90-day windows. These windows share transactions (by construction of rolling windows) and share the same account-level behavioral trajectory. Random sampling at the window level does not guarantee that windows from the same account are confined to the same split. A suspicious account's window from month 12 can land in training while its window from month 13 lands in test. This creates three leakage paths: (a) direct transaction overlap — the same transaction appears in both a training window and a test window, and the LSTM learns to memorize that transaction's embedding; (b) label correlation — a suspicious account at month 12 is very likely suspicious at month 13, so the model can partially recover the test label from training-set accounts rather than from the sequence structure; (c) graph feature leakage — merchant graph embeddings are computed from the 'training window' but, if account windows bleed across splits, the 'training window' for graph purposes may include accounts nominally in the test set.

This is distinct from simple temporal leakage. The issue is account-level correlation across splits, not just future-information leakage. A sequence model with an attention mechanism or LSTM memory is especially sensitive to this because it can overfit account-specific transaction patterns seen during training.

What would constitute evidence: Run the experiment with and without account-level blocking (i.e., hold out all windows from 20% of accounts entirely). If metrics drop substantially under account-level blocking, the original design was inflating performance via account bleed. Alternatively, measure the fraction of test-set accounts that also appear in the training set — if that fraction is high (e.g., >50%), the leakage concern is material.

---

**Issue 2: The confound control for 'feature volume' is incomplete — the proposed model receives strictly more features than the baseline**

The specific claim: giving the baseline aggregated merchant graph statistics while the proposed model uses sequence + graph features isolates 'sequence structure' as the source of any observed gain.

Why this might be wrong: The proposed model receives (1) full transaction-level sequences, (2) per-transaction merchant graph features (degree centrality, clustering coefficient, community membership for each counterparty), AND (3) temporal statistics. The baseline receives aggregate-level merchant graph statistics (average degree, fraction of high-degree counterparties). These are not equivalent representations of the same information — the proposed model sees the graph features at per-transaction granularity and can learn interaction effects between, say, a spike in amount and elevated counterparty centrality at a specific moment. The baseline sees only a marginal summary. Any performance gap is therefore attributable to at least three confounded factors: (a) sequence structure, (b) richer graph feature granularity, and (c) interaction effects between time and graph topology. The experiment cannot distinguish these.

A clean isolation of 'sequence structure' would require a third condition: a non-sequential model (e.g., XGBoost) trained on per-transaction features aggregated in the same way the LSTM processes them, or equivalently, the baseline given per-transaction graph features concatenated as a flat feature vector. Without this, the experiment establishes that 'sequence model + fine-grained graph features' beats 'aggregate model + coarse graph features' — which is a weaker and less interesting claim.

What would constitute evidence: A three-way comparison — (a) baseline with current aggregated features, (b) baseline with per-transaction features flattened/aggregated at the same granularity the LSTM receives, (c) LSTM. If (b) matches (c), the win is not from sequence modeling. If (b) beats (a) substantially, the graph granularity was the confound.

---

**Issue 3: Precision at fixed 85% recall is measured from a validation-selected threshold — this threshold is not fixed for test evaluation and may not generalize**

The specific claim: precision at 85% recall is the primary metric, measured on the held-out test set, reflecting operational objectives.

Why this might be wrong: The threshold that achieves exactly 85% recall on the validation set will almost certainly not achieve exactly 85% recall on the test set, due to distributional variation and finite-sample noise. The methodology does not specify how the operating threshold is determined for test evaluation. Three interpretations exist: (a) use the validation-set threshold directly on the test set — this will yield a recall that differs from 85%, potentially substantially if prevalence shifts; (b) find the test-set threshold that achieves 85% recall on the test set — this requires observing test labels to select the threshold, which is a form of test-set overfitting; (c) report precision at the empirical recall closest to 85% on the test set — this is operationally inconsistent and hard to compare across models. In an imbalanced AML dataset (typical prevalence 0.1%–2%), small shifts in prevalence between validation and test (even under stratification) cause the precision-recall curve to shift materially, meaning the threshold transfer from validation to test is unreliable.

This is not merely a precision issue — it affects the validity of McNemar's test, which compares model predictions at a fixed threshold. If each model's threshold is determined differently, the test's assumptions are violated.

What would constitute evidence: The protocol should specify the threshold-transfer rule explicitly and report the achieved recall on the test set alongside precision. If achieved recall deviates from 85% by more than ±2 percentage points, the primary metric comparison is inconsistent across models. Reporting the full precision-recall curve with AUC-PR as a threshold-free alternative would resolve this.

---

**Near-miss: Label conservatism (unlabeled = not suspicious)**

Labeling sequences without a confirmed case as 'not suspicious' conflates 'not yet investigated,' 'investigated but inconclusive,' and 'genuinely clean.' This introduces label noise among negatives. However, this is a known and ubiquitous limitation of AML datasets, and the methodology acknowledges it. It affects both models equally and does not create a differential bias favoring the proposed model over the baseline. It is a real limitation but not a methodological flaw that undermines the comparative experiment.

---

**Summary:** The three issues above are genuine methodological flaws. Issue 1 (account-level leakage via rolling-window random split) is the most severe because it inflates all reported metrics and disproportionately benefits the sequence model, which is more capable of memorizing account-specific patterns. Issue 2 (confounded feature granularity) undermines the core interpretive claim. Issue 3 (threshold underspecification for the primary metric) makes the primary metric comparison ambiguous in practice.
all_issues_raised: Rolling-window random split allows the same account to appear in both train and test sets, creating account-level label correlation and transaction overlap leakage that disproportionately inflates sequence model performance.

The proposed model receives per-transaction graph features while the baseline receives only aggregated graph statistics, confounding 'sequence structure' with 'feature granularity' as the source of any observed gain.

The primary metric (precision at fixed 85% recall) requires a threshold-transfer rule from validation to test that is unspecified; the threshold that achieves 85% recall on validation will not achieve 85% on test, making the metric comparison inconsistent and invalidating McNemar's test assumptions.
all_issues_adjudicated: Rolling-window random split allows the same account to appear in both train and test sets, creating account-level label correlation and transaction overlap leakage that disproportionately inflates sequence model performance.

The proposed model receives per-transaction graph features while the baseline receives only aggregated graph statistics, confounding 'sequence structure' with 'feature granularity' as the source of any observed gain.

The primary metric (precision at fixed 85% recall) requires a threshold-transfer rule from validation to test that is unspecified; the threshold that achieves 85% recall on validation will not achieve 85% on test, making the metric comparison inconsistent and invalidating McNemar's test assumptions.
